item.name: User Attributes
item.description:
User attributes capture standard (**system**) and organization-defined (**custom**) fields.
System attributes are built-in (`preset: true`); custom attributes are org-defined
(`preset: false`) and referenced by keys like `custom-attribute-93cbf`.

**Important notes**
- **Mandatory** attributes (`mandatory: true`) must be provided on user create/update.
- **Select fields:** use the `value` from the attribute's `attribute_options` array.
- **Boolean fields:** only `true`/`false` are accepted.
- **Dates** accept `MMM DD, YYYY` (e.g. `Jan 02, 2022`) or `YYYY-MM-DD` (e.g. `2024-01-15`).

**Data types** (`data_type`)

| Type | Example values | Use cases |
| ---- | -------------- | --------- |
| `text` | "Engineering" | Names, descriptions, free text |
| `number` | 5, 8.5 | Grades, ratings, quantities |
| `date` | "2024-01-15" | Hire/birth dates, deadlines |
| `boolean` | true, false | Yes/no flags |
| `single-select` | "Meets Expectations" | Single choice from options |
| `multiple-select` | ["JavaScript","Python"] | Multiple choices from options |

description.content: Returns all user attributes (system + custom), including field configuration, validation rules, and options.
Added as a part of security scheme: apikey

item.name: Users
item.description:
Manage users, their profile fields, status, and permissions.

**Custom attributes:** beyond the system fields, you can set organization-defined custom
attributes on create/update. Use each attribute's `attribute_key` as the field name
(discover them via `GET /beta/user-attributes`). System and custom attributes can be mixed
in one request — either nested under `custom_attributes` or as individual
`custom-attribute-*` fields. Example:

```json
{
  "first_name": "Jane",
  "last_name": "Smith",
  "email": "jane.smith@company.com",
  "departments": ["dept-123"],
  "custom-attribute-93cbf": "Product Management",
  "custom-attribute-d3b49": 9
}
```

item.name: Learning
item.description:
Courses, playlists, their content hierarchy, and learner reporting. These endpoints are served at `https://api.engagedly.com/beta/learning/` and are **read-only**. Two identifiers are the stable join keys for mapping to your own data: `short_code` (human-readable course code) and `learning_resource_id` (internal course ID; returned as `id` on the resource itself).

### Filtering & sorting (`filter_hash`, Learner Report)

`filter_hash` is a JSON object: `filter` maps each column to a payload whose shape depends on the column's data type, and `sort` takes a `field_name` + `order` (`asc`/`desc`). See the API Reference for the full per-column filter shapes and sortable fields.

OpenAPI description of the Engagedly API — replicating the hand-authored API Reference.

**Downloads:** [OpenAPI spec](openapi.yaml) · [Postman collection](engagedly-api.postman_collection.json)

## Authentication

Every request is authenticated with a **Client Key** and **Secret Key**, sent as the
`ClientKey` and `SecretKey` request headers. Keys are generated by a **Site Administrator**
under *Integrations → Engagedly API*. You must be a Site Administrator to generate them, and
the keys carry the permissions of the user who generated them. **The secret key is shown only
once — copy it when you generate it.** The API does **not** support CORS.

## Requests

All requests specify an API version in the URL. The current version is **beta**; the base
endpoint is `https://api.engagedly.com/beta/`. All requests must use HTTPS (plain HTTP fails).
`POST`/`PUT`/`PATCH` bodies are JSON and require `Content-Type: application/json` (otherwise
the API returns `415 Unsupported Media Type`).

## Responses & pagination

Successful responses are JSON with a `success: true` flag. List endpoints are paginated via
the `page` and `size` query parameters; responses include a `pagination` object with `page`,
`size`, `has_more`, and `total_records`.

## HTTP status codes

| Code | Meaning |
| ---- | ------- |
| 200 | OK — everything worked as expected. |
| 201 | Created — one or more resources were created. |
| 204 | No Content — succeeded, no response body. |
| 400 | Bad Request — malformed or missing parameter. |
| 401 | Unauthorized — invalid auth token. |
| 402 | Request Failed — parameters valid but the request failed. |
| 403 | Forbidden — insufficient permission, or not a user of the organization. |
| 404 | Not Found — wrong URL, or the resource has moved. |
| 405 | Method Not Allowed — HTTP method not supported for the resource. |
| 406 | Not Acceptable — requested response type unsupported. |
| 409 | Conflict — conflicts with another request. |
| 415 | Unsupported Media Type — missing `application/json` content type on POST/PUT/PATCH. |
| 422 | Unprocessable Entity — validation error creating/modifying a resource. |
| 429 | Too Many Requests — too many requests within a time frame. |
| 500 | Server Error — something went wrong on Engagedly's end. |

## Errors

Errors return a body with an `error_type` and an `errors` array.

**Error types**

| Type | Description |
| ---- | ----------- |
| `api_connection_error` | Could not connect to the Engagedly API. |
| `authentication_error` | Could not authenticate. |
| `invalid_request_error` | The request had invalid parameters. |
| `validation_error` | Field validation failed. |

**Error codes** (in `errors[].code`)

| Code | Description |
| ---- | ----------- |
| `missing_field` | A mandatory attribute is missing from the request. |
| `duplicate_value` | The submitted value already exists. |
